title: Project: Terminal Map
summary: Build a small folder map using pwd, ls, cd, mkdir, and touch.

sketchBlocks.title: Plan a folder map before you build it
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
A terminal project gets easier when you picture the structure first. In this project, you are acting like someone organizing a tiny field-notes workspace for a park map.

You will build a nested folder structure, then move into it and inspect it. The key idea is that `mkdir` creates folders, `touch` creates empty files, and `ls` helps you confirm what exists.

Here is a small example structure:

```bash
mkdir -p park-map/trails park-map/notes
touch park-map/notes/todo.txt
ls park-map
```

Line by line:

- First, `mkdir -p park-map/trails park-map/notes` creates the main folder and both subfolders in one step.
- Second, `touch park-map/notes/todo.txt` creates an empty note file inside `notes`.
- Third, `ls park-map` lists what is inside `park-map`, so you can verify the structure.

This matters because a good terminal user does not guess. They create a structure, then check it.

sketchBlocks.title: Move through the map safely
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
After you create folders, you need to move through them with `cd` and confirm your location with `pwd`.

A short navigation example looks like this:

```bash
cd park-map
pwd
cd trails
pwd
cd ..
```

Step by step:

- `cd park-map` moves into the `park-map` folder.
- `pwd` prints your current location so you can confirm where you are.
- `cd trails` moves one level deeper into the `trails` folder.
- The next `pwd` shows that deeper location.
- `cd ..` moves back up one folder level.

The symbols matter:

- `.` means the current folder.
- `..` means the parent folder.
- `~` means your home location.

For this project, the most useful safe move is `cd ..` when you need to go back up one level.

sketchBlocks.title: Inspect the finished workspace
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
A finished terminal task is not just about creating files. You also need to inspect the result.

You can use `ls` to list items and `pwd` to confirm location. For example:

```bash
cd park-map/notes
pwd
ls
```

Line by line:

- `cd park-map/notes` moves directly into the `notes` folder.
- `pwd` prints the full current location.
- `ls` lists the contents of `notes` so you can check whether the expected file is there.

Inspection is part of the skill. If you can create a structure but cannot verify it, you are still missing an important terminal habit.
